sn46.55:1.2: For Saṅgārava, see note on SN 7.21:1.2. | This discourse, without the final section on the awakening-factors, is also at AN 5.193.
sn46.55:2.1: Chāndogya Upaniṣad 6.7 describes an experiment in fasting. After not eating for fifteen days, the mantras are forgotten.
sn46.55:2.2: Saṅgārava seems to be a corrupted form of Sanskrit Śārṅgarava. A sage of that name features in the Mahābhārata and Kalidasa’s Śakuntalā as a student of the Vedic sage Kaṇva. Further, the Rig Veda Anukramaṇī identifies four “Śārṅgas” as the author of Rig Veda 10.142, an identification based on the four birds of that name in Mahābhārata 1.220–25. While we cannot identify this brahmin with any confidence, we can say it was a name current among Vedic sages. The root meaning of śārṅga is “horn”, from where it is applied to birds as possessing a horn, i.e. a beak. Since rava is an animal’s cry, this suggests the meaning “bird-cry”, an apt name for a poet.
